exemplar.title: âTo what extent has Unilever Indonesia's media campaign promoting personal care and beauty products impacted the company's revenue growth?â

detailedExemplarData.title: âTo what extent has Unilever Indonesia's media campaign promoting personal care and beauty products impacted the company's revenue growth?â

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, well-focused and consistently anchors each section of the investigation.
The introduction provides sufficient background context (market-size figures, product-segment share) to explain why the study matters.
Sources and business-analysis tools are justified, showing some informed selection.
weaknesses: Explanation is occasionally repetitive and statistics are sometimes inserted without immediate citation.
The methodology relies solely on secondary, company-generated sources with little triangulation from independent data.
Range of methods is adequate but not fully appropriate or effective for deeper insight.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall performance falls within the 3â4 band. Topic communication is adequate with sufficient explanation (band 3â4), the research question is exceptionally clear and well-focused (band 5â6) but the methodology remains only generally relevant with limited triangulation (band 3â4). Balancing these strands yields a final score of 4.

strengths: Core business and marketing frameworks (4Ps, SWOT, Porterâs Five Forces) are applied coherently to the revenue-growth argument.
Subject knowledge is clear and conceptually sound, demonstrating understanding of interrelated concepts such as advertising ROI and economies of scale.
Terminology and quantitative formulas (percentage-change calculations) are generally accurate and appropriately used.
weaknesses: Several key data points derive from descriptive PR material and lack external validation.
Some statistics (e.g., â73% of salesâ) appear without inline citation, weakening source application.
A few tables/images serve illustrative purposes rather than analytical ones, and some SWOT entries are incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application of source material, subject knowledge and use of terminology consistently meet the 3â4 band: mostly appropriate and coherent but with lapses in citation and depth. Thus, the work merits a score of 4 in Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Research assembles a coherent variety of company reports and market articles clearly relevant to the research question.
Analysis goes beyond description, linking growth-rate calculations to strategic frameworks with well-supported conclusions.
The conclusion acknowledges data limitations and external factors, displaying partially critical evaluation.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on company disclosures limits the exploration of independent or primary data to strengthen causation claims.
Assumptions around advertisingârevenue causality are not fully interrogated, and inflation/market factors receive only brief treatment.
Counter-arguments and alternative explanations (e.g., product launches, pricing strategies) are touched on but not rigorously evaluated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strandâresearch quality, analysis depth, and discussion/evaluationâaligns with band 7â9 (âgood research,â âclear analysis,â âwell-developed argument with partial critiqueâ). While not yet in the top band (10â12), the investigation demonstrates robust critical thinking meriting an 8.

strengths: The report follows the expected EE structure (title page, contents, introduction, analysis, conclusion, bibliography).
Headings/subheadings align with the table of contents, and figures are integrated into the narrative.
Basic Harvard referencing, pagination, and a word count are present.
weaknesses: Inconsistent figure numbering (multiple âFigure 3âs) and broken URLs detract from clarity.
The table of contents lacks page numbers, reducing navigability.
SWOT matrix formatting varies between pages, and some tables spill over margins.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets all requirements (band 3â4) but layout and formatting are only partially correct (band 1â2). Averaging these strands places Presentation at band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The question â âTo what extent has Unilever Indonesiaâs media campaign â¦ impacted the companyâs revenue growth?â â is explicitly stated and directly shapes every major section (4Ps, SWOT, financial analysis).  It is narrow enough (one firm, one country, one set of products, one dependent variable) to be addressed within 4 000 words, and the later analysis repeatedly connects back to revenue growth.  Focus could still be sharpened by isolating a specific campaign or time window, so the strand is strong but not flawless.

The conclusion recognises data limitations and external factors, showing some evaluative thinking.  However, the argument still leans on correlation, and counter-arguments or alternative explanations (e.g., product launches, pricing shifts) are only touched upon.  Evaluation is therefore partially critical but not âexcellentâ.

The essay applies core BM knowledgeâmarketing mix, SWOT, Porter, revenue-growth mathsâin a coherent way and demonstrates understanding of how these frameworks interrelate.  Weaknesses arise when the student equates correlation with causation and when financial data are interpreted without adjusting for inflation, yet overall knowledge is clear and mostly well supported.

Key terms such as âmedia campaignâ, ânet salesâ, âadvertising investmentâ, âbargaining powerâ and âeconomies of scaleâ are used accurately throughout, and formulas for percentage change are correctly presented.  Minor slips (e.g., confusing Figure numbers, occasional syntax issues) prevent the work from being entirely consistent, but terminology is generally sound.

The student calculates advertising-spend growth and revenue-growth rates and links them to analytical frameworks, thereby moving beyond description.  Assumptions (e.g., linking a 12 % swing in growth solely to advertising) are not fully interrogated, and inflation or market-wide factors are only briefly noted, so the analysis is clear but not sophisticated.

Most quotations, figures and tables are relevant to the marketingârevenue link and are used inside the argument (e.g., % advertising of net sales, growth calculations).  Yet several sources are essentially descriptive PR material from Unilever, and some images are illustrative rather than analytical.  The application is therefore mostly, but not consistently, appropriate.

The student relies entirely on secondary sources (company reports, Statista, press releases) and explains why primary data were unobtainable.  Business tools are pre-selected and justified, showing some informed choice.  However, the range is weighted toward company-generated information and there is little triangulation with independent financial or consumer data, so the methodology is adequate rather than fully âappropriate and effectiveâ.

A reasonable variety of annual reports, financial tables and market articles are assembled and the bulk of the evidence (advertising spend, revenue figures, market-share data) is relevant to the RQ.  The absence of primary data and the heavy dependence on company disclosures limit depth, yet the research clearly underpins the discussion.

Pagination, word count, and basic Harvard-style referencing are included, and visuals are integrated.  Nonetheless, figure numbering is inconsistent (two different Figure 3s), some tables spill over margins, and several URLs are broken or repeated, indicating only partial adherence to formatting conventions.

The title, research question and contextual background are all stated in the first pages and the introduction clearly explains why media campaigns matter to Unilever Indonesia.  The student supplies market-size figures, product-segment share, and the link between advertising and revenue, giving the reader sufficient rationale for the investigation.  Nonetheless, the explanation is occasionally repetitive and some statistics (e.g., â73 % of salesâ) are inserted without immediate citation, so the communication is solid rather than fully polished or comprehensive.

The essay follows the IB EE structure (title page, contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, works cited, appendix).  Headings and sub-headings match the table of contents, figures are numbered, and analytical sections flow logically.  All required components are present.

annotations.comment: Methodology relies solely on secondary Unilever sources; consider incorporating primary data or independent industry reports to triangulate findings.
The bullet describing revenue growth analysis is vague (ââ¦is analyzed to calculate the revenue growth rateâ); rephrase to clearly explain calculation steps and rationale.
Figure 1 is presented without interpretive commentary linking its content back to the research question; deepen your analysis of this visual.
Figure numbering is inconsistent (multiple Figure 3s appear); ensure sequential, unique numbering for all figures.
The case of Glow & Lovely CSR campaigns is described but lacks performance metrics; include data on sales impact or engagement results.
The inclusion of a precise word count at the start confirms adherence to IB formatting requirements.
The research question is clearly articulated and effectively grounds the entire investigation.
The table of contents lacks page numbers, making navigation less intuitive; please include them to improve presentation clarity.
The statistic â73% of the companyâs overall salesâ appears without an immediate citation; add an inline reference to strengthen source application.
The introduction implies that media spend directly causes revenue growth without controlling for other factors; clarify correlation versus causation.
The marketing banner âACHIEVE YOUR BRIGHTNESS MAKE YOUR DREAMS COME TRUE!â appears without context; explain its relevance or remove it.
Two distinct images share the label Figure 3; revise captions to maintain clear, sequential labelling.
The SWOT table lists âMedia campaigns enhance brandâ but the entry is cut off; complete this point to clarify its impact.
The SWOT matrixâs layout varies between pages, reducing readability; apply a consistent format for all quadrants.
The SWOT opportunities section ends mid-thought; expand your argument to fully explain revenue implications.
Accessed dates in citations vary in format; adopt a consistent date convention across all references.
The threats discussion on domestic competition truncates (âwhich mightâ); complete this sentence and assess its effect on revenue growth.
Porterâs Five Forces lists each force descriptively but lacks critical comparison of their relative intensity; weigh and prioritise forces.
Rewrite the percentage change formula with clear grouping: $$\frac{(2{,}315{,}686 - 1{,}323{,}599)}{1{,}323{,}599} \times 100$$ for readability and precision.
The revenue growth formula contains a typo in the denominator â39.545,959â; correct to $39{,}545{,}959$ to maintain numerical accuracy.
The discussion of inflation effects ends abruptly; specify the percentage increase and link it to revenue fluctuations.
The conclusion highlights correlation but lacks proposals for establishing causation; suggest adding methodological approaches to address this gap.
Reference entries in âWorks Citedâ lack uniform Harvard formatting; standardize italicization, date placement, and punctuation.
Several URLs in the works cited are broken across lines; ensure hyperlinks are continuous and correctly formatted.
